front: Accumulated value of 1 after n years at effective annual rate i (compound interest)
latexFront: AV \text{ of } 1 \text{ after } n \text{ years at rate } i
mnemonic: Compound = repeated multiplication; n factors of (1+i).
source: ASM FM / standard theory of interest

front: Discount factor v in terms of effective interest rate i
back: v = 1/(1+i), so present value of 1 one year hence is v
mnemonic: v 'voids' one year of interest growth.

front: Effective annual discount rate d in terms of i
mnemonic: Discount is interest paid up front, so it is i scaled down by (1+i).

front: i in terms of d
mnemonic: Invert the d = i/(1+i) relationship.

front: Key identity linking i and d (difference equals product)
back: i - d = id (equivalently d = i - id)
mnemonic: The gap between i and d is exactly their product.

front: Effective rate i in terms of nominal interest rate i^(m) compounded m times per year
mnemonic: Per-period rate is i^(m)/m, applied m times.

front: Effective rate relation for nominal discount d^(m) compounded m times per year
back: 1 - d = (1 - d^(m)/m)^m, equivalently v = (1 - d^(m)/m)^m
mnemonic: Mirror of the nominal-interest formula with minus signs.

front: Relationship between nominal interest i^(m) and nominal discount d^(m) at the same frequency m
mnemonic: Per-period accumulation and per-period discount are reciprocals.

front: Force of interest δ as the limit of nominal rates (relation to i)
back: δ = ln(1 + i); equivalently 1 + i = e^δ and v = e^(-δ)
mnemonic: Force = log of the annual growth factor; continuous compounding.

front: Force of interest δ_t from a general accumulation function a(t)
back: δ_t = a'(t)/a(t) = d/dt [ln a(t)]
mnemonic: Force is the instantaneous relative growth rate of the fund.

front: Accumulation function a(t) recovered from a (possibly time-varying) force of interest δ_s
back: a(t) = exp( ∫_0^t δ_s ds )
mnemonic: Integrate the force, then exponentiate.

front: Ordering of the equivalent annual rates d^(m), d, δ, i, i^(m)
back: d < d^(m) < δ < i^(m) < i (for m > 1, i > 0); all measure the same growth over a year
mnemonic: Discount rates below δ, interest rates above; more frequent compounding pulls toward δ.

front: Simple interest accumulation function over t years at rate i
back: a(t) = 1 + i·t (interest not earned on prior interest)
mnemonic: Linear growth, not exponential; force of interest decreases over time.

front: Present value of a single payment of amount C due in n periods at rate i
back: PV = C·v^n = C/(1+i)^n
latexFront: PV \text{ of } C \text{ at time } n
mnemonic: Discount each future dollar back by v per period.

front: Equation of value (definition)
back: An equation setting the value of inflows equal to the value of outflows, all accumulated/discounted to a common comparison date; basis for solving for unknown rate, time, or payment.
mnemonic: Pick one comparison date; everything migrates there.

front: Rule of 72 (approximate doubling time)
back: Time for money to double ≈ 72 / (100i); a quick mental estimate.
mnemonic: Exact is ln 2 / ln(1+i); 72 is the handy approximation.

front: Present value of an annuity-immediate: n payments of 1 at end of each period
back: a-angle-n = (1 - v^n)/i
mnemonic: Immediate = payments at period-end; divide the discount drop by i.

front: Accumulated value of an annuity-immediate: n payments of 1 at end of each period, valued at time n
back: s-angle-n = ((1+i)^n - 1)/i
mnemonic: s-angle-n = a-angle-n · (1+i)^n (accumulate the PV forward).

front: Present value of an annuity-due: n payments of 1 at start of each period
back: ä-angle-n = (1 - v^n)/d = a-angle-n · (1+i)
mnemonic: Due = paid one period earlier, so multiply immediate by (1+i); divide by d not i.

front: Accumulated value of an annuity-due: n payments of 1 at start of each period
back: s̈-angle-n = ((1+i)^n - 1)/d = s-angle-n · (1+i)
mnemonic: Due AV = immediate AV times (1+i); divide by d.

front: Link between annuity-due and annuity-immediate PVs (off-by-one identities)
back: ä-angle-n = 1 + a-angle-(n-1); and a-angle-n = v + a-angle-(n-1) ... key identity: ä-angle-n = a-angle-n + 1 - v^n
mnemonic: Due adds a payment at time 0 and drops the one at time n.

front: Present value of an n-payment annuity-immediate deferred m periods (payments start at time m+1)
back: v^m · a-angle-n = a-angle-(m+n) - a-angle-m
mnemonic: Value the annuity, then discount m extra periods; or subtract the first-m-payments slice.

front: Present value of a perpetuity-immediate (level payments of 1 forever, end of period)
back: a-angle-∞ = 1/i

front: Present value of a perpetuity-due (level payments of 1 forever, start of period)
back: ä-angle-∞ = 1/d
mnemonic: Due perpetuity = immediate perpetuity + extra payment at time 0 = 1/i + 1 = 1/d.

front: Present value of an increasing annuity-immediate (Ia): payments 1, 2, ..., n
back: (Ia)-angle-n = (ä-angle-n - n·v^n)/i
mnemonic: Numerator uses the DUE annuity minus the final n·v^n; divide by i.

front: Present value of a decreasing annuity-immediate (Da): payments n, n-1, ..., 1
back: (Da)-angle-n = (n - a-angle-n)/i
mnemonic: (Ia) + (Da) = (n+1)·a-angle-n; the decreasing version uses (n - a-angle-n).

front: PV of a perpetuity-immediate with payments increasing by 1 each year (1, 2, 3, ...)
mnemonic: Limit of the increasing-annuity formula as n→∞.

front: PV of a geometric annuity-immediate: first payment 1, each subsequent payment grows by factor (1+g)
back: PV = (1 - ((1+g)/(1+i))^n) / (i - g), for i ≠ g; equals n·v when i = g
mnemonic: It is a level annuity at the 'net rate' j where 1+j = (1+i)/(1+g).

front: PV of a geometric (growing) perpetuity-immediate: first payment 1, growth g, valuation rate i (i > g)
back: PV = 1/(i - g)
mnemonic: Gordon growth model; only converges when i > g.

front: PV of a continuous annuity paying at rate 1 per year for n years
back: a-bar-angle-n = (1 - v^n)/δ
mnemonic: Same numerator as a-angle-n, but divide by the force δ instead of i.

front: Accumulated value of a continuous annuity paying at rate 1 per year for n years
back: s-bar-angle-n = ((1+i)^n - 1)/δ
mnemonic: Continuous-payment analogue of s-angle-n; divide by δ.

front: PV of a continuously increasing continuous annuity (payment rate t at time t) over n years
back: (I-bar a-bar)-angle-n = (a-bar-angle-n - n·v^n)/δ
mnemonic: Continuous analogue of (Ia): numerator uses a-bar, divide by δ.

front: Sum identity linking increasing and decreasing annuities
back: (Ia)-angle-n + (Da)-angle-n = (n + 1)·a-angle-n
mnemonic: Each period's two payments sum to (n+1), a level (n+1) annuity.

front: PV of an annuity payable m-thly: n years of payments totaling 1 per year, paid 1/m at each m-th of a year (immediate)
back: a-angle-n^(m) = (1 - v^n)/i^(m)
mnemonic: Same numerator; divide by the matching nominal rate i^(m).

front: Outstanding loan balance after t payments — prospective method
back: B_t = (level payment P) · a-angle-(n-t): present value of remaining payments
mnemonic: Prospective = look forward; PV of payments still to be made.

front: Outstanding loan balance after t payments — retrospective method
back: B_t = L·(1+i)^t - P·s-angle-t: original loan accumulated, minus payments accumulated
mnemonic: Retrospective = look back; accumulate loan, subtract accumulated payments.

front: Interest portion of the t-th level loan payment (amortization)
mnemonic: Interest = rate × prior balance.

front: Principal portion of the t-th level loan payment (amortization)
back: P_t = P - I_t = P·v^(n-t+1)
mnemonic: Principal repaid grows geometrically by (1+i) each period: P_(t+1) = P_t·(1+i).

front: How successive principal repayments behave in a level-payment amortized loan
back: Principal repaid increases geometrically: P_(t+1) = P_t·(1+i); interest portions decrease.
mnemonic: Early payments are mostly interest; late payments mostly principal.

front: Sinking fund method: total periodic outlay when borrower pays lender interest at rate i and deposits into a sinking fund earning rate j
back: Total = L·i (interest to lender) + L/s-angle-n at rate j (sinking-fund deposit)
mnemonic: Pay the lender interest each period; separately accumulate the principal in a side fund.

front: Sinking fund: when does total cost equal a standard amortized loan?
back: If the sinking fund rate j equals the loan rate i, the sinking-fund method and amortization give identical total periodic payments.
mnemonic: Different rates is the whole point; equal rates collapse to amortization.

front: Basic bond price formula (general)
back: P = F·r·a-angle-n + C·v^n, where Fr = coupon, C = redemption value, i = yield, n = number of coupons
mnemonic: Price = PV of coupons + PV of redemption.

front: Premium/discount bond price formula (rewrites price relative to redemption value C)
back: P = C + (Fr - Ci)·a-angle-n; premium if Fr > Ci, discount if Fr < Ci
mnemonic: Premium/discount = (coupon − yield-on-redemption) annuitized.

front: When is a bond priced at a premium vs. a discount?
back: Premium (P > C) when coupon rate > yield rate (Fr > Ci). Discount (P < C) when coupon rate < yield rate. Par when equal.
mnemonic: High coupon relative to market yield = you pay extra (premium).

front: Write-up / write-down (amortization of premium or accumulation of discount) in coupon period t — amount of book value change
back: Book value change in period t = (Fr - Ci)·v^(n-t+1); premium amortized down, discount accreted up
mnemonic: Same geometric pattern as loan principal; negative for premium (write-down).

front: Coupon split: interest earned vs. principal adjustment in coupon period t (premium bond)
back: Interest earned = i·B_(t-1); amount of premium amortized = coupon − interest earned = Fr − i·B_(t-1)
mnemonic: Coupon = interest at yield + return of premium; mirrors loan amortization.

front: Makeham's formula for bond price
back: P = K + (g/i)·(C - K), where K = C·v^n (PV of redemption) and g = Fr/C (modified coupon rate)
latexBack: P = K + \dfrac{g}{i}\,(C - K),\quad K = C v^{n},\ g = \dfrac{Fr}{C}
mnemonic: Splits price into PV of redemption (K) plus PV of coupon stream expressed via g/i.

front: Callable bond pricing logic (how to price to guarantee a minimum yield)
back: Price the bond at every possible call/redemption date at the desired yield and take the LOWEST price. Rule of thumb: if priced at a premium, assume the EARLIEST call date; if at a discount, assume the LATEST date.
mnemonic: Issuer calls when it benefits them; price defensively at the worst-case (lowest-price) date.

front: Modified coupon rate g (definition, in bond notation)
back: g = Fr/C: the coupon amount expressed as a rate on the redemption value C (rather than on face F). Equal to the coupon rate r when C = F.
mnemonic: g rebases the coupon onto C; used in Makeham and premium/discount analysis.

front: Price of a bond between coupon dates: dirty (full) price t into a coupon period (theoretical/semi-theoretical)
back: Dirty price = B_t·(1+i)^t; clean price = dirty price − accrued coupon (Fr·t under practical/linear method)
mnemonic: Dirty = clean + accrued interest; quotes are usually clean.

front: Yield rate (internal rate of return) of a series of cash flows — definition
back: The rate i that makes the net present value of all cash flows (in and out) equal to zero.
mnemonic: Set NPV = 0 and solve for i.

front: Net present value (NPV) of a project's cash flows at rate i
back: NPV = Σ C_t·v^t (positive inflows, negative outflows); accept project if NPV ≥ 0
mnemonic: Discount every cash flow to time 0 and sum.

front: Dollar-weighted vs. time-weighted rate of return (distinction)
back: Dollar-weighted = IRR over the period, sensitive to timing/size of deposits & withdrawals (approx: I / (A + contributions weighted by time)). Time-weighted = product of (1 + sub-period returns) − 1, removes the effect of cash-flow timing.
mnemonic: Time-weighted chains sub-period returns (fund-manager skill); dollar-weighted is the actual IRR.

front: Dollar-weighted (simple-interest approximation) rate of return over a year
back: i ≈ I / (A + Σ C_t·(1 - t)), where A = beginning balance, I = interest earned, C_t = net deposit at time t (fraction of year)
mnemonic: Weight each deposit by the fraction of the year it was invested.

front: Spot rate s_t (definition)
back: The annualized yield on a zero-coupon bond / single payment maturing at time t; the rate used to discount a single cash flow at time t: PV = C/(1 + s_t)^t.
mnemonic: Spot = today's rate locked in for a single maturity t.

front: Relationship between spot rates and the (one-period) forward rate from time t to t+1
mnemonic: No-arbitrage: investing to t+1 directly = investing to t then rolling forward one period.

front: General forward rate over the interval [t, t+k] derived from spot rates
mnemonic: Forward = ratio of long accumulation to short accumulation, annualized over k.

front: Macaulay duration of a series of cash flows
back: D_Mac = (Σ t·C_t·v^t) / (Σ C_t·v^t): PV-weighted average time of the cash flows
mnemonic: Weighted-average maturity, weights = each cash flow's share of PV.

front: Modified duration and its relation to Macaulay duration
back: D_mod = -P'(i)/P(i) = D_Mac/(1+i) = D_Mac·v
mnemonic: Modified = Macaulay discounted one period; it is the sensitivity dP/P per unit i.

front: First-order price-change approximation using modified duration
back: ΔP ≈ -P·D_mod·Δi; i.e. P(i + Δi) ≈ P(i)·(1 - D_mod·Δi)
mnemonic: Price falls (approximately linearly) as rates rise; slope is modified duration.

front: Macaulay duration of a level perpetuity-immediate
back: D_Mac = (1+i)/i
mnemonic: A handy closed form often tested directly.

front: Macaulay convexity of a series of cash flows
back: C_Mac = (Σ t^2·C_t·v^t)/(Σ C_t·v^t): PV-weighted average of t^2
mnemonic: Like duration but weight by t-squared; captures curvature.

front: Modified convexity (definition via second derivative of price)
back: C_mod = P''(i)/P(i)
mnemonic: Second-order term; improves the duration-only price approximation.

front: Second-order (duration + convexity) price-change approximation
back: ΔP ≈ -P·D_mod·Δi + (1/2)·P·C_mod·(Δi)^2
mnemonic: Taylor expansion of P(i): linear duration term + quadratic convexity term.

front: Redington immunization — the three conditions
back: 1) PV(assets) = PV(liabilities). 2) Duration of assets = duration of liabilities (equivalently P'_A = P'_L). 3) Convexity of assets > convexity of liabilities (P''_A > P''_L).
latexBack: PV_A = PV_L,\quad D_A = D_L,\quad C_A > C_L
mnemonic: Match value, match duration (slope), out-convex the liabilities (curvature) — protects against small rate moves.

front: Full (exact / dedication) immunization vs. Redington immunization
back: Full immunization protects against rate changes of ANY size, typically by surrounding each liability with one asset cash flow before and one after (matched in PV and duration). Redington protects only against SMALL changes (local condition).
mnemonic: Full = global protection (bracket each liability); Redington = local protection.

front: Cash-flow matching (dedication) — definition
back: Constructing an asset portfolio whose cash inflows exactly match the timing and amount of each liability outflow, eliminating interest-rate risk entirely without relying on duration/convexity conditions.
mnemonic: The brute-force, zero-reinvestment-risk approach.
